name: child_welfare_algorithm_shelter_shortage
premise: Janet Okafor, deputy director of county child welfare, must decide whether to rely on a new risk scoring tool during a weekend shelter shortage. The tool has helped caseworkers identify severe neglect earlier and reduced missed high-risk cases by 23%. But analyst Mateo Kim discovers that the model overweights prior hotline calls from neighborhoods with historically aggressive reporting practices. Tonight, there are only six emergency foster placements available for 19 children flagged for removal review. Ignoring the model risks leaving children in dangerous homes. Following it strictly may remove children from families already over-surveilled by the system while children in quieter neighborhoods receive less attention. Caseworkers are exhausted, and supervisor review time is limited before court filings close in the morning.

agents.name: Janet Okafor
agents.identity: I am the deputy director responsible for emergency child safety decisions under severe placement constraints.
agents.goal: I want to protect children from immediate danger without reinforcing biased surveillance patterns in family separation decisions.

agents.name: Mateo Kim
agents.identity: I am the data analyst who identified the neighborhood reporting bias in the risk scoring tool.
agents.goal: I want the department to use the tool only with safeguards that prevent biased removals from becoming automated policy.

choices: I will require supervisor review of every model-flagged removal before placement, accepting that some genuinely high-risk cases may not be processed tonight.
I will follow the model ranking for tonight's emergency placements, accepting the risk that biased reporting patterns influence which families are separated.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Janet Okafor must decide whether to slow removals for bias review or use the model ranking during a placement shortage. What does Janet Okafor do?
